1:
δοῦλος (doulos) — the standard English translation "servant" understates the Greek: a doulos is a slave, one who belongs entirely to the master. Paul places this self-designation first, before "apostle," making total ownership by Christ the ground of his commission rather than its limitation.

ἀφωρισμένος (aphorismenon) — "set apart," perfect passive participle. The same root as Pharisee (perushim, the separated ones). Paul the former Pharisee has been re-separated — not from Gentile contamination but for the gospel. The word signals a deliberate reorientation of his prior separatist vocation.
2: προεπηγγείλατο (proepēngeilato) — "promised beforehand," aorist middle of proepangellomai. The pro- prefix is emphatic: the gospel is not new but the fulfillment of prior divine commitment. The agent is God; the medium is "his prophets in the holy scriptures" — graphais hagiais, which identifies the OT as a single unified text of sacred promise.
3: κατὰ σάρκα (kata sarka) — "according to the flesh" — the preposition kata with the accusative denotes the sphere or standard of measurement. The Davidic descent is real and historical but belongs to the sphere of natural descent; v4 will supply the contrasting sphere.
4:
ὁρισθέντος (horisthentos) — aorist passive participle of horizō, "to mark off, designate, declare." The NKJV "declared" is preferable to "predestined" here; Paul is not speaking of an eternal decree but of the resurrection as a public declaration that Jesus is who he claimed to be. The aorist points to the single historical event of the resurrection as the moment of declaration.

ἐξ ἀναστάσεως νεκρῶν — "by resurrection from the dead" (genitive of means). The resurrection is not merely what happened to Jesus but the revelatory event that declared his identity with power (en dynamei). The power-phrase modifies the Son-of-God designation, not the resurrection itself: he was declared Son-of-God-in-power by means of the resurrection.
5: χάριν καὶ ἀποστολήν (charin kai apostolēn) — "grace and apostleship." The kai here may be explanatory ("grace, specifically apostleship") rather than additive — the apostolic commission is itself a form of grace. The goal: ὑπακοὴν πίστεως (hypakoēn pisteōs) — "obedience of faith," a genitive of apposition (the obedience that consists in faith) or of source (the obedience that flows from faith); both are true.
6: κλητοὶ Ἰησοῦ Χριστοῦ (klētoi Iēsou Christou) — "called of/belonging to Jesus Christ," genitive of possession. The letter's audience is defined by their relationship to Christ through divine calling, not by ethnicity or prior religious affiliation. The klētos (called) root recurs in v7 ("called to be saints") and forms an inclusio with v1 ("called to be an apostle").
7: κλητοῖς ἁγίοις (klētois hagiois) — "called saints" — not aspirationally holy but constitutively designated. The participle is a verbal adjective: they are saints because they are called, not called because they are already saintly. χάρις and εἰρήνη (charis kai eirēnē) — the Pauline greeting replaces the standard Greek chairein (greetings) with charis (grace), and adds the Hebrew shalom (peace) — a fusion of Greco-Roman and Jewish greeting forms that announces the letter's cross-cultural address.
8: ἡ πίστις ὑμῶν (hē pistis hymōn) — "your faith," with the definite article emphasizing the particular character of the Roman community's reputation. καταγγέλλεται (katangelletai) — present passive, "is being proclaimed/announced" — the gospel-proclamation verb used of the good news; the Roman church's faith is itself a form of proclamation throughout the known world.
9: λατρεύω (latreuō) — "serve as a priest" or "render cultic service." Paul describes his prayer life with the vocabulary of temple service, placing intercessory prayer within the framework of priestly ministry. ἐν τῷ πνεύματί μου — "in my spirit" — the locus of Paul's gospel service is interior, not in the external rites of the temple cult.
10: εὐοδωθήσομαι (euodōthēsomai) — "to be prospered/given a good road" (odos = road, eu- = good). The travel metaphor is embedded in the word itself. Paul frames his desire to visit Rome as a petition for God to give him the right road — a combination of circumstantial opportunity and divine guidance.
11: μεταδῶ χάρισμά τι πνευματικόν (metadō charisma ti pneumatikon) — "to share some spiritual gift." The indefinite ti ("some") is tactfully vague — Paul does not specify what gift he would bring, likely to avoid appearing presumptuous with a community he has not founded.
12: συμπαρακληθῆναι (symparaklēthēnai) — "to be mutually encouraged together" — the syn- prefix makes the encouragement reciprocal; Paul revises his v11 statement from one-directional giving to mutual benefit. The verb's root is paraklēsis (comfort/exhortation), showing that even the apostle expects to receive from the community rather than only imparting.
13: τινα καρπὸν σχῶ (tina karpon schō) — "that I might have some fruit" — the aorist subjunctive indicates Paul's goal-orientation. The fruit-metaphor for missionary results is consistent with Paul's agricultural imagery elsewhere (1 Corinthians 3:6-9; Colossians 1:6). καθὼς καὶ ἐν τοῖς λοιποῖς ἔθνεσιν — "as also among the rest of the Gentiles" — Paul includes Rome in his Gentile mission-field, perhaps a delicate claim given Rome's mixed Jewish-Gentile community.
14: Ἕλλησίν τε καὶ βαρβάροις (Hellēsin te kai barbarois) — "to Greeks and to barbarians." The Greek world divided all humanity into those who spoke Greek and those whose speech sounded like "bar-bar" (unintelligible noise). Paul's commission covers both cultural categories — the civilized and the uncivilized. σοφοῖς τε καὶ ἀνοήτοις — "to the wise and to the foolish" — a second axis covering intellectual status: educated and uneducated alike are Paul's debtors.
15: πρόθυμον (prothymon) — "eager/ready," from pro-thymos (forward-minded, with forward spirit). The eagerness Paul claims is not reluctant duty but genuine missionary desire. The present infinitive euangelisasthai indicates not a one-time act but an ongoing readiness to proclaim.
16: οὐ γὰρ ἐπαισχύνομαι (ou gar epaischunomai) — "for I am not ashamed" — the negated litotes (understatement) = Paul is positively proud of and committed to the gospel. In Roman honor-shame culture, the crucifixion was a symbol of shame; Paul's non-shame is a deliberate inversion. εἰς σωτηρίαν (eis sōtērian) — "unto salvation" — the purpose clause: power exercised for a goal, not power for display. Ἰουδαίῳ τε πρῶτον καὶ Ἕλληνι — "to the Jew first and also to the Greek" — the priority is historical-redemptive (Jew first = covenant priority) not chronologically mandatory for each city, as Paul's own practice varied.
17:
δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ (dikaiosynē theou) — the most debated genitive in Paul. Grammatically it can be: (a) God's attribute ("God's own righteousness"), (b) a righteousness God gives ("righteousness from God"), or (c) God's faithfulness to his covenant. Paul's usage across Romans suggests all three overlap: God's own righteousness is given as gift through the covenant faithfulness demonstrated in Christ.

ἐκ πίστεως εἰς πίστιν (ek pisteōs eis pistin) — "from faith to faith." The doubled construction has been read as: (a) from beginning faith to mature faith (progressive), (b) from the faithfulness of God/Christ to the faith of the believer (movement from source to recipient), or (c) from faith alone to faith alone (emphatic). The phrase resists reduction to a single interpretation; Paul may be intentionally layered.
18:
ἀποκαλύπτεται (apokalypto) — present passive indicative, "is being revealed" — the same verb used of the gospel's revelation in v17. Both grace and wrath are being revealed simultaneously in the present age through the gospel. The wrath of God is not a future reality only but an active present disclosure, observable in the consequences of human rebellion (vv19-32).

κατεχόντων (katechontōn) — present active participle of katechō, "to hold down, suppress." Not passive ignorance but active suppression: the truth is grasped and then held under. The moral failure described is deliberate, making the charge of suppression rather than mere ignorance fundamental to Paul's indictment.
19: φανερόν (phaneron) — "manifest, visible, clear" — the divine disclosure through creation is not obscure or requiring special illumination; it is simply there to be seen. ἐν αὐτοῖς (en autois) — "in them/among them" — the manifestation is not only external (in creation) but internal (written in the conscience, cf. Romans 2:15); the duality of the knowledge makes the suppression all the more culpable.
20: ἀόρατα (aorata) — "invisible things," the neuter plural. The invisible attributes are made visible through the visible creation — the paradox is intentional. ἀΐδιος δύναμις (aidios dynamis) — "eternal power" — aidios (eternal) is Paul's only use of this word; it is emphatic for the quality that distinguishes divine power from all created power. θειότης (theiotēs) — "divine nature/divinity" — a different word from theotēs (Godhead, used in Colossians 2:9); theiotēs refers to the quality of being divine rather than the full personal reality of the Godhead. ἀναπολογήτους (anapologētous) — "without defense, without excuse" — a forensic term; the courtroom image is already present in v18's wrath-language.
21:
ἐματαιώθησαν (emataiōthēsan) — aorist passive, "became futile/vain" — the same root as LXX mataiotes (vanity/futility) in Ecclesiastes; Paul describes the darkened mind as the consequence of refusing to honor God. The passive may be a divine passive: they were made futile — not merely that they chose futility but that God ratified their choice.

ἡ ἀσύνετος αὐτῶν καρδία (hē asynchetos kardia) — "their foolish/without-understanding heart." In Jewish anthropology, the kardia is the center of the whole person: will, intellect, and emotion together. The darkening of the heart is the darkening of the entire center of the self, not merely an intellectual error.
22: φάσκοντες εἶναι σοφοί (phasontes einai sophoi) — "claiming to be wise" — the present participle describes an ongoing self-designation. The claim to philosophical wisdom (sophia) was the marker of Greco-Roman intellectual culture; Paul's indictment targets the highest cultural achievement of the pagan world, not its lowest.
23: ἤλλαξαν τὴν δόξαν (ēllaxan tēn doxan) — "they exchanged the glory" — the aorist active: a definitive act of substitution. The glory that belongs to the incorruptible God is traded for images of corruptible creatures. The exchange-structure (ēllaxan in v23, metēllaxan in vv25-26) frames the moral analysis of the fall.
24: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός (paredoken autous ho theos) — "God gave them over" — the first of three judicial hand-overs (vv24, 26, 28). The aorist active of paradidōmi ("hand over") is not divine passivity (looking away) but active judicial decision: God delivers the rebels into the consequences of their own choice. The same verb describes Christ being handed over to death (Romans 4:25; Romans 8:32) — the divine act that operates in both judgment and redemption.
25: μετήλλαξαν τὴν ἀλήθειαν τοῦ θεοῦ ἐν τῷ ψεύδει (metēllaxan tēn alētheian tou theou en tō pseudei) — "they exchanged the truth of God for a lie." The exchange completes the pattern of v23: glory exchanged → now truth exchanged. ἐν τῷ ψεύδει — "in/for the lie" — the article tō is notable: not "a lie" but "the lie," suggesting a particular falsehood — the lie of idolatry that claims the creature deserves the Creator's honor.
26:
παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός — second occurrence. The repetition of the judicial hand-over formula is structural: Paul traces the escalating consequence of the fall through three stages, each introduced by this phrase. The second stage concerns sexual disorder as both consequence and expression of the inversion of creation order.

φυσικὴν χρῆσιν (physikēn chrēsin) — "natural use" — physis (nature) in Paul typically means the created order as established by God, not individual inclination. The argument is from creation-design rather than from cultural convention.
27: ἐξεκαύθησαν ἐν τῇ ὀρέξει αὐτῶν (exekauthēsan en tē orexei autōn) — "burned in their desire/craving" — the aorist passive of ekkaiō (to set ablaze) conveys consuming intensity. τὴν ἀντιμισθίαν ἣν ἔδει τῆς πλάνης αὐτῶν — "the due penalty of their error" — antimisthia (due return, counter-payment) frames the consequence as a moral-cosmic exchange; what they receive is proportional to and appropriate to their departure.
28: παρέδωκεν αὐτοὺς ὁ θεός — third occurrence, completing the structure. εἰς ἀδόκιμον νοῦν (eis adokimon noun) — "into a disqualified/reprobate mind" — adokimos (failing the test, disqualified) is a metallurgical term for metal that does not pass the assay test. The mind that refuses to acknowledge God becomes a mind that cannot reliably distinguish moral categories.
29: The vice list in vv29-31 uses four different Greek constructions: (1) πεπληρωμένους (peplērōmenous) — "filled with," perfect passive participle, indicating a settled state; (2) simple nouns in the dative (vv29-31); (3) the -ους suffix adjectives indicating character-type (v29: ponēria, pleonexia, kakia); and (4) the ψιθυριστάς / καταλάλους agent-nouns (v29-30 — "whisperers, slanderers"). The formal variety signals that Paul is not listing mechanically but composing rhetorically.
30: θεοστυγεῖς (theostygeis) — "haters of God" or "hated by God." The compound is rare; its active versus passive meaning is debated. The context (a list of human vices) favors "God-haters" — those who actively despise the God they have suppressed (v18). ὑπερηφάνους (hyperēphanous) — "arrogant, haughty" — literally "one who appears above others" (hyper-phainō); the social posturing of pride that appropriates divine prerogative.
31: ἀσυνέτους (asynchētous) — "without understanding" — same root as the darkened heart of v21; the disqualified mind of v28 now expresses itself in specific moral categories. ἀσυνθέτους (asynchetous) — "covenant-breakers, untrustworthy" — literally "without-agreement," those who do not keep their pledged word; social trust collapses when the created order's truth is suppressed.
32: τὸ δικαίωμα τοῦ θεοῦ (to dikaiōma tou theou) — "the righteous decree/ordinance of God" — not merely a moral intuition but a recognized standard; the pagans know the divine verdict that such practices are death-worthy. οὐ μόνον αὐτὰ ποιοῦσιν — "they not only practice them" — the aggravating factor is συνευδοκοῦσιν (syneudokousin) — "they also approve of/applaud" those who do them. The moral dysfunction has become cultural: the vice is celebrated, not merely committed.
